Also from [lsof code](https://fossies.org/dox/lsof_4.89_src/aix_2dproc_8c_source.html), line 476. This code is tricky but it's correct. We're reading the processes into `p` in `PROCINFO_INCR` increments. We `realloc` the next "block" for `processes` every time we read a full `PROCINFO_INCR` block. The last call can return less than `PROCINFO_INCR` processes, but we still read that block and need to go over it, so `np` must be increased by the number of processes in the last block - which is in `i`. See the [docs for `getprocs64`](https://www.ibm.com/support/knowledgecenter/en/ssw_aix_61/com.ibm.aix.basetrf1/getprocs.htm)

Precisely, starting from real images is a common practice, whereas starting from random states is not common. 

Let's see if the following arguments make sense: The negative examples in the training loop all start from real images. The training loop may never see some true negatives from uniform random choices, because the number of visited states is far fewer than the 2^130 possibilities. As a result, swapping the sampling seed with a uniform random start may have unforeseeable outcomes.

As a matter of fact, I did the experiment with random pixel values sampled from an iid beta distribution. More than half of the images did not converge to images of digits.

In the paper I referenced, the authors trained an VAE using real images. However, visualization of the latent states reveals large areas that are not visited by the real images. Sampling from them would likely generate unreal digits. I believe this is a common issue with generative models.

It may be worth breaking this test out to rely on yield for when this test is ran. > Does this and below need to exist if they're just pass?

Yes. So the way this works is that we have two Python classes, `CustomDevelop` and `CustomInstall` that inherit from `setuptools`' `develop` and `install` command classes respectively, to override the `develop` and `install` commands (e.g., `python setup.py develop` and `python setup.py install`). These classes should define the method `run`, in which we put our custom behavior (in this case, running the `Install Certificates.command` before our package is installed).

Since we want to do the same thing, whether we're installing in dev mode or normal mode, and to avoid code duplication, the decorator `install_certs` takes these classes, and essentially [overrides their `run` methods with our custom one](https://github.com/cs50/submit50/blob/9ea6aef9284cf1d1b4bae017dd039d053431f1c9/setup.py#L24).

We then [pass](https://github.com/cs50/submit50/blob/9ea6aef9284cf1d1b4bae017dd039d053431f1c9/setup.py#L49) these classes as _command classes_ to `setup`, in order to override the `develop` and `install` commands.

I actually hesitated while implementing.

The main argument that push to let `description` at the top level is that in [Matti's comment](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/pull/681#issuecomment-404348529), `description` seem to perfectly fit the definition of a "fully-specified and normalised metadata":

- We have no interrogation about it, and we use it in many places, including on our [`parameters/`](https://fr.openfisca.org/api/v21/parameters) API route.
- `parameter.description` has always been exposed

Now, if `description` is at top level, why not `reference`?

It's arguable, but if everyone is convinced that references are needed, it seems like we are not fully sure about what a reference should _be_ exactly.  And whenever we get a better idea about that, moving `reference` from `metadata` to top level is not even a breaking change for Core.

Looking at the internals of `random.sample` we won't get any performance gains from this and are likely to take a performance hit (a little).  Easier to read though... I'm now leaning towards `-1` on using `random.sample` here.

However, I do think this implementation needs to be changed as this could take a **lot** of iterations if `len(all_bucket_nodes)` is large and `count` is also large as it will start to encounter more and more nodes it's already seen.

I came up with this solution.  Let me know what you think.

```python
def get_random_sample(self, count):
    all_node_idx_paths = [
        (bucket_idx, node_idx)
        for bucket_idx in range(len(self.buckets))
        for node_idx in range(len(self.buckets[bucket_idx].nodes))
    ]
    random.shuffle(all_node_idx_paths)
    selected_node_idx_paths = all_node_idx_paths[:count]
    return [
        self.buckets[bucket_idx].nodes[node_idx]
        for bucket_idx, node_idx
        in selected_node_idx_paths
    ]
```

that out in advance.

hashable: all primitive types and enums are hashable, lists, maps and sets
aren't, and structs and unions are hashable only if all their elements are
hashable.

The GraphQL type system by default assumes that types are nullable. We don't currently use the non-null type annotations anywhere in the compiler, so we just blindly strip them everywhere.

Primer on GraphQL types
- `[String]`: nullable list of nullable strings
- `[String!]`: nullable list of non-nullable strings
- `[String]!`: non-nullable list of nullable strings
- `[String!]!`: non-nullable list of non-nullable strings

For message types, we should absolutely stop nesting.

For enums, I would argue that we should nest unless we want to inject the individual enum identifiers into the global/package namespace.

For the example above, when I had it at the top level, the identifier STRING shows up as a onnx.STRING in python.  It's actually worse - if you look at AttributeType and the DataType enum from TensorProto, we would have two conflicting identifiers (that would at best accidentally have the same value).

I think our style should be NO nesting of messages and ALWAYS nesting of enums.  I'd leave IR_VERSION as is just for historical reasons.
